workflow.purpose:
Orchestrate coding standards generation through environment detection, user interview, and document writing. Produces `.docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md` — a prescriptive, paradigm-aware coding standards document tailored to the project.
Brownfield projects: detect language/paradigm/frameworks from codebase, then interview. Greenfield projects: gather tech choices from user interview directly.
workflow.mandatory-context: Read all files referenced by the invoking command's execution-context before starting. Also read any document or text passed as parameter ($ARGUMENTS) in the invoking command.

process.step:
**MANDATORY FIRST STEP — Execute environment detection before any user interaction:**
```bash INIT=$(node ~/.claude/agile-context-engineering/src/ace-tools.js init coding-standards) ```
Parse JSON for: `commit_docs`, `is_brownfield`, `is_greenfield`, `has_existing_code`, `has_package_file`, `has_coding_standards`, `wiki_dir_exists`, `has_system_architecture`, `has_system_structure`, `has_git`.
Also resolve the mapper model for spawning the wiki-mapper agent: ```bash MAPPER_MODEL=$(node ~/.claude/agile-context-engineering/src/ace-tools.js resolve-model ace-code-wiki-mapper --raw) ```
Display stage banner:
``` ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ ACE > Coding Standards ║ ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ ```
**If `has_coding_standards` is true:**
Use AskUserQuestion: - header: "Standards exist" - question: "Coding standards already exist at `.docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md`. What would you like to do?" - options: - "Update" — Refine the existing standards through a new interview - "Start fresh" — Discard and create new standards from scratch - "Skip" — Keep the current standards as-is
If "Update": Read existing file, hold as seed context, continue to step 2 If "Start fresh": Ignore existing file, continue to step 2 If "Skip": Exit workflow
**If `has_git` is false:** Initialize git: ```bash git init ```

**If `is_greenfield` is true:**
There is no codebase to analyze. Skip detection entirely — the interview (step 3) will gather language, paradigm, and framework preferences directly from the user.
Hold detection results as: `DETECTION = null` (signals greenfield to step 3).
Continue directly to step 3 (interview).
**If `is_brownfield` is true:**
Spawn a detection agent to analyze the existing codebase. Use a Task agent to avoid filling main context with file scanning:
``` Task( prompt="Analyze this project's technical profile for coding standards generation.
**Step 1 — Detect languages:** Check for: package.json, tsconfig.json, *.csproj, *.sln, Cargo.toml, go.mod, pyproject.toml, setup.py, requirements.txt, build.gradle, pom.xml, Gemfile, mix.exs, *.cabal, pubspec.yaml, Package.swift, composer.json
**Step 2 — Detect paradigm:** Scan 5-10 representative source files. Classify as: - OOP-heavy: classes, interfaces, DI containers, inheritance - Functional-heavy: pure functions, pipelines, algebraic types, pattern matching - Multi-paradigm: mix of both - Systems: ownership, manual memory, error-by-return
**Step 3 — Detect frameworks and tooling:** Check package files and imports for: React, Angular, Vue, Express, ASP.NET, Spring, Django, Phoenix, Gin, Actix, Jest, xUnit, pytest, etc. Check for: .eslintrc, .prettierrc, ruff.toml, .editorconfig, biome.json
**Step 4 — Detect existing standards:** Check for existing CLAUDE.md, .docs/ standards, lint configs, .editorconfig
**Step 5 — Detect project structure patterns:** Find where constants, config, DI registration, and shared code live.
**Return format — structured summary only:** LANGUAGES: [list with confidence] PARADIGM: [OOP / Functional / Multi-paradigm / Systems] FRAMEWORKS: [list] TOOLING: [linters, formatters, configs found] EXISTING_STANDARDS: [any existing standards docs found] CONSTANTS_LOCATION: [where constants/config values live] DI_LOCATION: [where dependency injection is configured, if applicable] PATTERNS_OBSERVED: [3-5 key patterns seen in the code]", subagent_type="Explore", model="{MAPPER_MODEL}", description="Detect project technical profile" ) ```
Hold detection results for the interview step.

Follow the questioning guide from `questioning.xml`. You are a thinking partner helping the user articulate their coding philosophy.
**Opening — adapt based on greenfield vs brownfield:**
If BROWNFIELD (detection results available): ``` I analyzed your project. Here's what I found:
- Language(s): [detected] - Paradigm: [detected] - Frameworks: [detected] - Existing tooling: [linters/formatters found] [If existing standards found: "You already have [doc] — I'll build on that."]
Let me understand your coding standards preferences. ```
If GREENFIELD (no detection results): ``` This is a greenfield project — no existing code to analyze yet. I'll need to understand your plans to create the right standards. ```
Then ask about the tech stack using AskUserQuestion: - header: "Language" - question: "What language(s) will this project use?" - options: (pick the 3-4 most common, based on context if any) e.g., "TypeScript", "Python", "C#", "Go"
Follow up with paradigm preference: - header: "Paradigm" - question: "What programming paradigm will you primarily use?" - options adapt to the language chosen: - For TypeScript: "Class-based OOP", "Functional", "React components + hooks" - For Python: "OOP with classes", "Functional/procedural", "Mixed" - For C#/Java: "OOP with SOLID" (only realistic option, confirm don't ask) - For Go: "Go idioms" (Go has its own paradigm, confirm don't ask) - For Rust: "Rust idioms" (traits + ownership, confirm don't ask)
Then ask about frameworks if not already known.
**Key areas to explore (adapt based on conversation, don't checklist):**
1. **Strictness level** "How strict do you want the coding standards to be?" - "Enterprise-grade strict" — Zero tolerance for violations, every rule enforced - "Pragmatic but principled" — Strong guidelines with room for justified exceptions - "Minimal essentials" — Only rules that prevent real problems
2. **Pain points — THE MOST VALUABLE QUESTION** "What mistakes have you seen Claude or developers make repeatedly?" Follow up on every pain point. Each one becomes an emphatic rule. Examples they might mention: - "Claude puts multiple classes in one file" - "Claude swallows errors silently" - "Claude hardcodes values" - "Claude over-engineers simple things" - "Claude doesn't check what already exists"
3. **Paradigm preference** (if multi-paradigm language) Already gathered for greenfield; confirm for brownfield if multi-paradigm detected. - For Python: "OOP with classes" / "Functional/procedural" / "Mix" - For TypeScript: "Class-based OOP" / "Functional with types" / "React hooks" - For Kotlin/Scala: "OOP with interfaces" / "Functional with sealed classes" / "Mix"
4. **Architecture enforcement** "Do you want the standards to enforce architecture patterns?" - SOLID principles, Clean Architecture layers, DI requirements - Or just code-level rules without architectural mandates
5. **File organization rules** "One class/module per file strictly, or are small related types OK together?" "Max file length preference?"
6. **Error handling philosophy** "How should errors be handled?" - Fail fast with exceptions - Result/Either types for expected failures - Language-specific patterns
7. **Testability requirements** "Should the standards mandate testability patterns?" - Always code against interfaces/abstractions - Pure functions where possible - DI for all dependencies
8. **Custom rules** "Any specific rules from your experience that you want enforced?" Capture these verbatim — they are the user's hard-won lessons.
If the user provided a context document ($ARGUMENTS), absorb it first and use it to skip questions that are already answered. Probe gaps and ambiguities instead.
**Decision gate:**
When you understand their philosophy, pain points, and specific preferences: - header: "Ready?" - question: "I understand your coding philosophy. Ready to generate your coding standards?" - options: - "Generate standards" — Create the document - "Keep exploring" — I have more to share
Loop until "Generate standards" selected.

**Create directory structure:** ```bash mkdir -p .docs/wiki/system-wide ```
**Prepare a context brief (300-500 words) that distills:** - Project type: greenfield or brownfield - Detection results OR user-stated tech choices (languages, paradigm, frameworks, tooling) - User's strictness preference - User's pain points (listed explicitly — these become emphatic rules) - Paradigm preference and which template modules to include - Architecture enforcement preferences - File organization rules (construct per file, max length) - Error handling philosophy - Testability requirements - Custom rules (verbatim from user) - Constants location, DI config location (from detection or "TBD" for greenfield) - If updating existing standards: what to preserve vs. change
**Spawn the ace-code-wiki-mapper agent to write the document:**
``` Task( prompt="You are writing a prescriptive coding standards document for the project wiki.
**Context brief from detection + interview:** {paste the context brief here}
**Instructions:** 1. Read the coding standards template: ~/.claude/agile-context-engineering/templates/wiki/coding-standards.xml 2. Assemble the document following the template's assembly guidelines: - ALWAYS include universal-standards (adapt placeholders to the project's language) - Include paradigm modules based on context brief (OOP, functional, systems, or multiple) - Generate language-conventions section from detection results or user-stated choices - Include project-specific-rules from user's pain points and custom rules - For greenfield: use [TBD] markers for project-specific paths (constants dir, DI config) that will be filled once the codebase exists 3. If brownfield: explore the codebase to cross-reference actual file paths for constants directories, DI configuration, shared utilities, etc. 4. Tone: DIRECT and EMPHATIC. Bold for emphasis, caps for critical words. This is a mandate document, not suggestions. 5. Target length: 150-350 lines. Comprehensive but not bloated. 6. Write the file to `.docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md`
**Return format — ONLY this, nothing else:** DONE - Paradigm modules included: [list] - Total sections: [N] - Line count: [N] - Top 3 emphatic rules: [list the most critical rules]", subagent_type="ace-code-wiki-mapper", model="{MAPPER_MODEL}", description="Write coding standards document" ) ```

Show the agent's returned summary to the user. Then:
- header: "Standards" - question: "Coding standards written to `.docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md`. Does this look right? Review the full file in your editor for details." - options: - "Approve" — Looks good, commit it - "Adjust" — I want to change some things - "Redo interview" — Let's go back and discuss more
**If "Adjust":** - Ask what they want to change - Spawn the ace-code-wiki-mapper agent to adjust: ``` Task( prompt="Read `.docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md` and make these changes: {user's requested changes}. If changes reference codebase paths or patterns, explore the codebase to verify. Use the Edit tool to modify in place. Return only a confirmation of what changed.", subagent_type="ace-code-wiki-mapper", model="{MAPPER_MODEL}", description="Adjust coding standards" ) ``` - Present for review again. Loop until approved.
**If "Redo interview":** - Return to step 3 (interview), preserve detection results - Hold previous answers as context
**If "Approve":** Continue to commit step.

Commit the coding standards:
```bash git add .docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md git commit -m "docs: initialize coding standards" ```
Display completion:
``` ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ ACE > Coding Standards Created ║ ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
+ .docs/wiki/system-wide/coding-standards.md committed.
i These standards will be used as context by ACE commands when planning and executing stories.
Next > /ace:map-system Map your codebase structure and architecture. ```

workflow.success_criteria: - Init function executed (brownfield/greenfield detected, existing standards checked) - Brownfield: project technical profile detected via Explore agent - Greenfield: language, paradigm, frameworks gathered from user interview - User interviewed about coding philosophy and pain points - coding-standards.md written with appropriate paradigm modules - Document is prescriptive and emphatic, not descriptive - User reviewed and approved the document - Document committed to .docs/wiki/system-wide/
